role_summary: SNCC Atlanta national-office staff (1964-1966); Northern Coordinator of the Friends of SNCC chapter network and SNCC's fundraising under Executive Secretary James Forman; later a Baltimore community organizer.
ai_reading: Cosine similarity in this corpus puts Garman Robinson's passages in a tight cluster of SNCC organizers, with Timothy Jenkins (entry 120, count 210), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83, count 169), Maria Varela (entry 81, count 167), Dottie Zellner (entry 30, count 165, and her single highest passage similarity at 0.84), and Phil Hutchings (entry 98, count 160) as her top five neighbors by shared-passage count. Several were white SNCC staff or coalition-bridging figures, so the embedding picks out that subgroup rather than placing her with all SNCC staff equally. Two concept-axis positions complicate the picture in opposite directions. On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis the embedding reads her at +0.32, toward the Northern pole. That tracks her Northern-coordinator title, yet the substance of her testimony is Southern field detail: her Greenwood-office summer, her daily reporting on McComb, Albany, and Selma. The model keeps her with the coordinating desk rather than relocating her to the field sites those reports describe. On the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis she lands at -0.44, toward Collective Discipline, which converges with her Atlanta job of organization-building (allocations, fundraising, the chapter network); the model reads her testimony as operational, organization-scale language rather than as reflective conscience-narrative. The influence graph adds a convergence the cultural record has not foregrounded: the figure her interview names most is Judy Richardson (entry 69, out-edge count 9), who is also a top neighbor by passage similarity (entry 69, top score 0.76), and the two co-edited the SNCC women's anthology together.
biographical_paragraph: Betty Garman Robinson came to civil rights organizing from the Northern campus left, not from a Southern church basement [src: 2]. Born in New York City on January 8, 1939 and raised in the suburb of Pleasantville, she enrolled at Skidmore College, where after her first National Student Association congress in the summer of 1958 she switched her major from psychology to political science and ran campus forums against the period's anti-communist redbaiting [src: 2]. Graduate study at Berkeley put her inside Students for a Democratic Society, and she chaired the University of California Berkeley Friends of SNCC chapter; it was from that chapter that SNCC Executive Secretary James Forman recruited her [src: 2]. In March 1964 she moved to Atlanta to staff the SNCC national office, and by the fall of 1964 she had become Northern Coordinator, directing the Friends of SNCC department, the fundraising appeals, and the allocation of money to the field projects [src: 2, 3]. She co-edited the SNCC women's anthology Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (University of Illinois Press, 2010), contributing the chapter "Working in the Eye of the Social Movement Storm" [src: 4]. After leaving SNCC she settled in Washington and then moved to Baltimore in 1972 to work in a factory, where she married a Black factory worker and organized for the next half-century in community, labor, and racial-justice campaigns; she co-founded the Baltimore chapter of Showing Up for Racial Justice (SURJ) [src: 3]. She discusses fellow SNCC interviewees in the corpus including Judy Richardson (entry 69) and Courtland Cox (entry 24). She died in Baltimore on October 11, 2020 [src: 3]. Her oral history forms entry 7 of the Civil Rights History Project at the Library of Congress [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Betty Garman Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked why she chose SNCC over SDS, where she felt sidelined as a woman, Garman Robinson points to the recognition she found in the SNCC office:
interview_snippets.quote: So in the SNCC space, because I could take initiative, because I could rise to the occasion, I could, I could do something concrete, and I got affirmed by James Forman and by Casey that I was doing a good thing and that I was needed and necessary and important.

interview_snippets.speaker: Betty Garman Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: On her assignment during the 1964 Freedom Summer, before she became Northern Coordinator that fall:
interview_snippets.quote: So I'm the lead Northern Coordinator in the Greenwood office during summer 64.

interview_snippets.speaker: Betty Garman Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing the daily routine that connected the Atlanta office to the Northern support network:
interview_snippets.quote: we called all the Friends of SNCC offices every day. And we would give them a report on what had happened in McComb, Mississippi, what had happened in Greenwood, what had happened in Albany, what had happened in Selma.

interview_snippets.speaker: Betty Garman Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the framing she used when writing SNCC's fundraising appeals:
interview_snippets.quote: one of the things that we stayed away from was the, oh, you have to help the poor Mississippi person who has no rights. We were more, this is a democratic country. We need to be committed and dedicated to our ideals.

interview_snippets.speaker: Betty Garman Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: After the attack on marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, she used the office's resources to move organizers to Selma fast:
interview_snippets.quote: I was able to charter a plane to send four people, Ivanhoe Donaldson, Courtland Cox, and Prathia Hall, and I don't know who the fourth person was, to Selma from Atlanta.

interview_snippets.speaker: Judy Richardson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Judy Richardson, who worked beside Garman Robinson in the SNCC office, places her in the chain of Northern coordinators after Dinky Romilly:
interview_snippets.quote: she was also doing campus friends of SNCC until Betty Garman comes in, and she does the campus friends of SNCC.

movement_context: The Friends of SNCC chapter network Garman Robinson coordinated from the Atlanta national office was the principal fundraising and political-support infrastructure that financed SNCC's southern field operations through the 1964 Freedom Summer and the 1965 Selma campaign [src: 3]. James Forman's institutional design (he served as SNCC Executive Secretary from October 1961 through May 1966) placed the Atlanta office at the center of a small-donor financial-engine model: Friends of SNCC chapters operated in northern campus communities, liberal-left congregations, and peace-movement networks, raising the operating budget that kept the southern field staff on subsistence stipends [src: 5]. Garman Robinson's white-SNCC-staff position (her oral history is among the catalog's white-SNCC-staff testimonies the AI's-reading observation places her structurally adjacent to) was the racial configuration that the late-1966 Kingston Springs, Tennessee SNCC staff meeting (the meeting at which Stokely Carmichael was elected chair, the Black Power program was adopted, and the position of white SNCC staff was formally reconsidered) ended; her departure from the SNCC payroll in 1966 followed that institutional reorganization [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Garman Robinson moved to Washington after leaving SNCC, then to Baltimore in 1972, where she took factory work and organized a rank-and-file movement inside her union; she married a Black factory worker during those years [src: 3]. Over the following decades she worked in occupational-medicine and public-health programs at Johns Hopkins and then returned to full-time organizing, serving as lead organizer for the Citizens Planning and Housing Association on Baltimore neighborhood, housing, and anti-crime campaigns, and later co-founding the Baltimore chapter of Showing Up for Racial Justice (SURJ) [src: 3]. She co-edited Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (University of Illinois Press, 2010), the anthology of more than fifty first-person accounts by women in SNCC, and is recorded by the SNCC Legacy Project among the movement veterans it memorializes [src: 3, 4]. She died in Baltimore on October 11, 2020, at the age of eighty-one [src: 3].

gallery.caption: James Forman, SNCC Executive Secretary from October 1961 through May 1966 and Garman Robinson's direct supervisor during her March 1964 to 1966 tenure in the SNCC Atlanta national office. Photographed for the Portland Press Herald, May 9, 1969.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Portland Press Herald, May 9, 1969, Page 20)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964
gallery.caption: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964. Garman Robinson had taken her desk at the SNCC Atlanta national office a month earlier, in March 1964; the Friends of SNCC chapter network she coordinated was the principal Northern-campus channel through which Freedom Summer recruits learned about and applied to the project. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)
